Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd mostly study when I remembered I had something due or when the anxiety got bad enough that I couldn't ignore it anymore. I'd listen to lectures while doing other things around my apartment, like cooking or cleaning, since that's how I absorb information best. Sometimes I'd have these bursts where I'd do a ton of work in one sitting, then nothing for days.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go through phases where I'd be somewhat consistent for a week, then completely fall off. Deadlines were really the main thing that dictated when I studied. If something was due, I'd cram. If nothing was pressing, I'd drift away from coursework entirely and deal with other life stuff or just zone out.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or seeing a notification on my phone. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, which honestly made my anxiety worse most of the time. I rarely logged in just because I was curious or excited about the material. It felt more like an obligation I kept putting off.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural part of my day. I'd go through stretches where I'd forget it existed entirely. When I did use it regularly, it was because assignments were piling up and I had no choice. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do rather than something that fit into my life.
Q5: The recorded lectures were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things and actually retain information that way. The discussion boards were okay when other students explained things in simpler terms than the professor. But honestly, most of the resources felt like they required more energy than I had to give.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and extra materials. They required too much focused attention and I just couldn't bring myself to sit and read for long periods. The optional practice exercises felt pointless when I was already struggling to keep up with required work. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. Anything that felt abstract or like busywork just made me more bored and frustrated. I needed to feel like there was a clear payoff, otherwise my brain would just check out completely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. When you're already anxious about keeping up with the required stuff, adding more feels impossible. Required activities had this weight to them that forced me to act. Optional ones were easy to dismiss because there was no immediate consequence for skipping them.
Q9: Yeah, a lot actually. I'd download the audio from lectures and listen on my phone when I was out or trying to fall asleep. I took notes in a physical notebook because screens give me headaches after a while. I also used YouTube videos to understand concepts better because sometimes the course materials didn't click for me.
Q10: Most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd listen to downloaded lectures on repeat while commuting or doing chores. I'd review my handwritten notes for hours, but none of that shows up anywhere. The LMS would make it look like I barely engaged, but I was actually processing the material in my own way, just not online.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture or save the slides once, then never open them on the LMS again. I'd study from those saved files for days or weeks. It was just easier to have everything offline where I could access it without logging in and getting overwhelmed by all the notifications and deadlines staring at me.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. My anxiety would spike and I'd just freeze instead of doing anything productive. Financial stress was also a constant background noise that made it hard to focus on coursework. Some days I just felt too bored and disconnected to care about any of it.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing on my mind. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them instead of asking for help. Fatigue made everything feel impossible. I'd open the LMS, feel overwhelmed by everything I needed to do, and close it without doing anything.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd click through videos or scroll through readings without actually absorbing anything. My eyes would be on the screen but my mind was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, even though I knew I wasn't really learning.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew it would make my anxiety worse later. The assignments that seemed complicated or unclear were the worst. I'd avoid them until the last possible moment because starting felt too overwhelming. It's this weird cycle where avoiding things makes you more anxious, but the anxiety makes you avoid more.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS consistently. Without them, I'd drift completely. I'd log in frantically the day before something was due and try to cram everything in. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could force myself to engage. The pressure was the only thing cutting through the boredom.
Q17: Feedback helped when it was encouraging, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more. If I did poorly on something, I'd feel defeated and avoid the course for a while. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but it never lasted long. I needed more consistent encouragement to stay motivated.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid and didn't really accommodate different learning styles. Everything was set up for people who learn by reading and typing, not listening. The weekly modules felt repetitive and monotonous, which fed into my boredom. I would have engaged more if there was more variety or flexibility in how we could participate.
Q19: The lack of flexibility made engagement harder for me. Everything had to be done a certain way at a certain time. The mindfulness intervention they offered was actually somewhat helpful for my anxiety, but it didn't change the fundamental structure that wasn't working for me. More audio-based options would have made a huge difference.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need without finding it, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually submitting. Maybe they're confused about requirements and keep looking for clarity. Or they could be like me sometimes, opening things and zoning out without actually doing the work.
Q21: That's basically my situation. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the system. My assignment scores were actually really high because when I did engage, I did it thoroughly. The LMS activity doesn't capture listening to lectures on repeat while doing dishes or reviewing notes on the bus. Engagement isn't just clicks.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long pages were open, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention. They miss all the offline work, the thinking, the processing that happens away from the screen. They definitely can't capture emotional states like boredom or anxiety that affect how meaningfully you engage with what you're clicking on.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's when the material feels relevant and you want to understand it, not just get through it. For me, true engagement is rare. Most of the time I'm just surviving, doing what I need to pass without feeling any real connection to the content.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much my anxiety affected every interaction with the course. It can't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures offline or the mental energy it took just to log in some days. It doesn't know about my financial stress or how bored and disconnected I felt most of the time. The data shows actions, not the struggle behind them.
